# Audience path pages — full illustrative case studies (churches, nonprofits, institutions)

**Source of canonical facts and beats:** `src/components/path/data/case-study.churches.ts`, `case-study.nonprofits.ts`, `case-study.institutions.ts`. The narratives below **expand** that structured copy into long-form prose (~1,000 words each). They remain **fictional composites** (“based on real patterns”) — do not present them as named client engagements.

---

## Instructions for the AI Studio Agent (Google AI Studio / faithful site build)

When building or refreshing **`/churches`**, **`/nonprofits`**, and **`/institutions`**:

1. **Keep the page structure as it exists today:** `PathIntro` → sticky path (`PathStickySection` with Safety / Sandbox / Skills / Solutions) → **`CaseStudy`** (`id="case-study"`) → `PathFootnote` (and any closing CTAs already specified in the parent prompt).

2. **Do not replace the case study block with a summary.** At the **bottom** of each audience page — i.e. **within or immediately following** the case study region that already sits after the path — render the **full long-form narrative** for that audience from this document (the section titled **Churches**, **Nonprofits**, or **Institutions** below).

3. **Target length:** ~**1,000 words** per narrative (acceptable band **900–1,100**). If the layout uses a collapsible “Read the full case study,” the **default expanded content** must still be this full text (not an excerpt).

4. **Fidelity:** Preserve every **stat**, **named role/group**, **pull-quote meaning**, **list of experiments**, **boundaries**, and **ordered “why this worked” sequence** from the TypeScript sources. You may add connective prose and short scene-setting **only** where it does not contradict those facts.

5. **Design tokens:** Use the same semantic typography as the existing `CaseStudy` section (`Section` / `Container`, muted body, serif display accents per site recipes). No raw hex or marketing grays.

6. **Disclosure:** Keep visible labeling equivalent to **Case Study · Illustrative** (or clearer: “Illustrative composite — not a specific client”).

---

## Churches — Riverside Church

**Stats (display as today):** ~1,200 people · 14 staff (ministry, communications, operations) · 3+ pastoral domains (teaching, small groups, counseling).

**Title:** How a growing church introduced AI without compromising voice, pastoral care, or trust.

Riverside Church is not a real congregation with that name on a sign — it is a **fictional composite**, stitched together from patterns we see repeatedly in multi-staff churches: AI shows up in pockets long before it shows up on an agenda. Riverside is a growing church of about **1,200 people**, with **14 staff** spanning ministry, communications, and operations, and more than **three distinct pastoral domains** — teaching, small groups, and counseling — where “voice” and trust are not abstract ideas; they are the currency of the institution.

### The situation they were actually in

AI did not arrive at Riverside through a strategy meeting. It arrived in **staff workflows**, one person at a time. A communications staff member used ChatGPT to draft email announcements. A teaching pastor experimented with summarizing research notes. Someone on youth ministry used AI to generate discussion questions. No one had made a church-wide decision about AI — yet it was already in use: quietly, individually, and differently depending on the role. From the outside, Sunday mornings and midweek programs looked normal. From the inside, leadership felt a pressure that was hard to name until someone said it aloud: *What happens to our voice, our people, and our responsibility if we don’t lead this?*

That question is the hinge. Riverside’s story is not “we banned AI” or “we embraced AI.” It is: **we stopped improvising in isolation** and started leading with clarity.

### Step 01 · Safety — They clarified what they believed

Riverside chose not to begin with tools. They began with a conversation anchored by a single question: **What should AI never do here?** That question turned out to be more productive than any vendor demo.

A small leadership group took ownership: the **lead pastor**, **executive pastor**, **communications director**, and **one elder**. They worked through practical and theological terrain together: What is appropriate for AI to assist with? What must remain human-led? Where is spiritual responsibility genuinely involved? Where is efficiency appropriate — and where is it not?

The line that mattered most was around **teaching and pastoral voice**. They decided explicitly: **AI can assist preparation — but it cannot originate what is preached or personally spoken in pastoral care.** They wrote that down — not as legalism, but as a shared vow the staff could cite when uncertainty appeared.

Within about a month, the shift was internal more than external: a short, clear AI guideline existed; staff understood where AI fit and where it did not; sensitive zones — counseling, pastoral communication, prayer requests — were clearly off-limits; and leadership shared a single posture rather than four private theories. Nothing about Riverside’s public ministry changed overnight — but something crucial changed backstage: **the church had named its responsibility before scaling its tools.**

### Step 02 · Sandbox — They experimented intentionally

Boundaries in place, Riverside still did not “roll out AI.” They ran a **small set of intentional experiments**: drafting internal communications using anonymized content; generating first-pass small group questions **with human review**; summarizing research notes for sermon prep — explicitly **not** writing sermons. Each experiment had three non-negotiables: a clear boundary, a named owner, and a place where outcomes were shared.

Some experiments helped. Some did not. AI sped up internal communication drafts, but tone still needed a human editor. Small-group question drafts were sometimes useful as a starting point — and sometimes pastorally thin. Occasionally the output was technically fine but spiritually “off.” The breakthrough was not the win rate of any single tool — it was cultural: **staff stopped guessing individually and started learning collectively.**

### Step 03 · Skills — They trained for judgment, not just usage

By this point, staff were already touching AI; the goal was not introduction but **wisdom**. Training focused on when AI is appropriate in ministry contexts, when it should not be used, how to review outputs with discernment, and how to protect pastoral voice and integrity.

The curriculum mattered — but what mattered more was **what leadership modeled**. The lead pastor began naming decisions out loud: when AI had been helpful, when it had not, and where he drew personal lines. That visibility gave the staff something instructions alone cannot supply: **a living example of discernment.**

Over time, Riverside’s culture shifted: staff became more thoughtful, not merely faster; conversations about AI normalized; teams traded practices instead of hiding experiments. At a deeper level, the church was doing something formation-shaped: **forming people who could think — not just use tools.**

### Step 04 · Solutions — They built tools that fit their ministry

Only after Safety, Sandbox, and Skills did Riverside invest in anything custom. The tools reflected their actual context: a communications assistant trained on Riverside’s language and tone; a resource search tool spanning sermons, small-group materials, and internal documents; a planning assistant for recurring ministry rhythms.

They were equally explicit about what they refused to build: **no sermon-writing tools**, **no pastoral-care automation**, **nothing that replaced relational ministry**. Where care was at stake, they built something different in kind: **a discernment guide for staff and lay leaders — before using any tool at all.**

The payoff was not primarily velocity — it was **clarity**. Staff spent less time reinventing resources; communication grew more consistent; leaders fielded fewer repeating questions. Yet the heart of ministry stayed intact: **people still pastored people.**

### What this actually produced — and why the order held

Riverside did not rebranding itself as an “AI church.” It became **clearer about convictions**, **more aligned as a team**, and **more capable in daily work** — and it adopted AI **without quietly weakening** what matters most in a church.

Nothing about Riverside was unusual in scale or sophistication. The difference was **sequence**: clarify what matters, experiment carefully, form people, **then** build tools. That order is load-bearing — skip it, and tools outrun conscience; keep it, and technology serves mission instead of improvising around it.

---

## Nonprofits — Northbridge Youth Mission

**Stats:** 28 staff · ~$4.5M annual budget · 3+ sensitive programs (youth, counseling, detention ministry).

**Title:** How a mid-sized youth nonprofit brought AI under control — without losing trust.

Northbridge Youth Mission is a **fictional composite** patterned on real youth-serving nonprofits: camps, mentorship, counseling lines, and programs where minor consent, dignity, and donor trust intersect. Northbridge has **28 staff**, an annual budget of roughly **$4.5 million**, and **more than three sensitive program areas** — including youth-facing work, counseling-adjacent ministry, and programming tied to **juvenile detention**.

### The situation they were actually in

Before anything formal, AI was already in the building — not as strategy, not as malice, but as **quiet habit**. A development associate used ChatGPT to help draft donor emails. A program director tried summarizing notes with Claude. Someone on communications experimented with image tools. Nobody had ordered staff to stop — and nobody had blessed the behavior either. There was **no policy**, **no shared understanding**, and **no visibility** into who was doing what with which data.

Externally, grant reports still went out on time and camp seasons looked familiar. Internally, executives kept circling the same uneasy question: *Is this fine — or are we about to create problems we can’t see yet?*

Nonprofit leaders know this sensation: you are simultaneously proud of your team’s scrappiness and frightened by what scrappiness can hide — a draft email that accidentally echoes another organization’s appeal, a summary that wanders too close to identifiable detail, an image tool that violates consent norms you haven’t even named yet.

### Step 01 · Safety — They stopped guessing and made a decision

Northbridge began where Riverside did — not with a platform pick, but with a blunt question: **What are we actually okay with?** That question proved harder than anyone expected — because “okay” is not one setting across development, programs, HR, and crisis-facing ministries. Every department had a different risk surface; every experiment someone ran without telling anyone else quietly rewrote the organization’s exposure.

A leadership cell formed: the **CEO**, **COO**, **head of development**, and **a board member** accountable for governance. They pressed for crisp answers: Where is AI appropriate? Where is it not? What data can **never** be used? What always needs human review?

The hardest conversation centered on Northbridge’s mentorship program **inside juvenile detention**. In that room, abstraction collapsed into moral clarity: **We cannot have AI drafting or shaping communication with kids in custody.** That became a bright line — documented, communicated, and treated as non-negotiable.

Within about **six weeks**, the fog lifted: a short, readable AI policy existed; staff knew allowances and prohibitions; sensitive data categories were defined; leadership aligned on boundaries. The policy read like human sentences, not IT jargon — because the goal was shared judgment under pressure, not checkbox compliance when a deadline loomed.

Nothing flashy happened publicly — no ribbon-cutting for a new platform — but privately, something essential did: **everyone operated from the same understanding.** Donors still received appeals; youth still arrived at camp; the fragile trust inside detention mentorship gained an explicit fence rather than a vague hope.

### Step 02 · Sandbox — They experimented — but safely

Clear boundaries enabled disciplined curiosity. Northbridge still refused a “rollout.” Instead, they green-lit a short list of experiments: drafting donor newsletter sections from **anonymized** content; summarizing **program patterns** without real case identifiers; testing internal planning scenarios. Each trial required an owner, explicit **no-sensitive-data** guardrails, and a log of results.

Some efforts saved time; some misfired on voice; one promising idea proved **too risky** to continue once tradeoffs became visible. Sandbox discipline meant experiments ended with **stories**, not just screenshots — what was tried, what data never touched the model, what got edited before release, and what would never ship.

For the first time, the organization could articulate not only wins but **why** a dead end was dead: **a shared understanding of what was useful, what wasn’t, and what wasn’t worth the risk.** That collective memory became protection against the next shiny shortcut someone would inevitably discover at 10 p.m. before a grant deadline.

### Step 03 · Skills — They trained people — and shaped judgment

Staff were already using AI; the reframed question became: **How do we help people use it well?** Training went beyond prompting tricks to ethics-in-practice: when AI is appropriate, when it is not, how to review outputs before publication, how to protect organizational voice and donor trust.

Leaders refused cosplay competence. They modeled real-time judgment: *I used AI for this, but not for that.* *This crossed a line for me.* *I wasn’t sure, so I didn’t use it.* Over months, Northbridge’s culture edged from anxious secrecy toward disciplined openness: staff grew **more confident and more careful**, asked sharper questions, and pooled practices instead of hiding tabs.

AI ceased to be merely a private convenience and became **a shared discipline** — still imperfect, but no longer invisible.

Northbridge discovered something nonprofits often miss in vendor-led AI narratives: **capability is relational.** When judgment lives only in policies no one reads, tools amplify chaos. When judgment lives in repeated, modeled decisions, tools amplify coherence.

### Step 04 · Solutions — Then — and only then — they built tools

After Safety, Sandbox, and Skills, Northbridge commissioned narrow tools with obvious purposes: a donor communication assistant tuned to Northbridge’s voice; a searchable internal knowledge layer for staff resources; workflows supporting reporting and grant preparation.

For their highest-risk context — **mentorship in detention** — they rejected drafting automation entirely. In its place they invested in human-scale infrastructure: **a guide to help mentors learn how to think and show up — before using any tool at all.**

Coherence, not raw speed, became the measurable win: less reinventing the wheel, faster access to institutional memory, fewer bottlenecks around a single executive, and teams that felt **aligned** rather than parallel.

Custom tools did not absolve humans of moral imagination — they **channeled** it. The donor assistant still needed eyes on every send; the knowledge base still needed owners who curated rather than dumped documents; grant workflows still terminated in accountable signatures, not auto-generated claims.

### What this actually produced — and why the order held

Northbridge did not market itself as an “AI-powered nonprofit.” It grew **clearer about boundaries**, **more consistent operationally**, and **more capable as a team** — and it used AI **without slowly eroding trust** forged over decades.

Again, the institution wasn’t unique; **the sequence was**: decide what matters, experiment safely, build capability, **then** build tools. In nonprofits where one leaked prompt can wound real children or donors, order is not bureaucracy — it is stewardship.

---

## Institutions — Westbridge Seminary

**Stats:** ~600 students · 3 faculty divisions (theology, ministry, biblical studies) · 2 program tracks (residential and online).

**Title:** How a seminary approached AI without undermining formation, scholarship, or academic integrity.

Westbridge Seminary is a **fictional composite** graduate theological school — roughly **600 students**, faculty organized across **three divisions** (theology, ministry, biblical studies), and **two tracks** (residential and online). Its dilemma is the academy’s dilemma in miniature: AI entered through syllabi and stress before it entered policy.

### The situation they were actually in

AI did not knock as a memo from the provost; it arrived through **students**. Some used AI to draft papers; others for outlining or research summaries. Faculty awareness splintered: some professors banned AI outright, others ignored it, still others experimented privately with the same tools their students were told not to touch. From the registrar’s view, the term proceeded normally; inside seminar rooms, a quieter crisis gathered: *What does formation look like in a world where AI can produce words faster than students can think?*

### Step 01 · Safety — They defined their posture

Westbridge refused whack-a-mole enforcement. It sought **institutional belief** first — beginning with a deceptively simple question — **Is AI allowed?** — and quickly recognizing that simplicity was the wrong frame.

Residential students and online cohorts experienced different temptations: the former faced seminar norms and embodied community; the latter faced isolation, compressed timelines, and the lure of frictionless drafts at the end of a long workday. A one-line ban would not produce integrity across both tracks — only **shared clarity** could.

Faculty divisions amplified the tension: biblical studies worried about original-language nuance; practical theology worried about pastoral authenticity; systematic theology worried about argument integrity that looked coherent while resting on sand.

A working group convened: the **academic dean**, **several faculty members**, **one student representative**, and **IT leadership**. They pressed harder questions: What counts as **authorship**? Where is assistance legitimate? Where does AI **short-circuit formation**? What must **never** be delegated?

The breakthrough conviction was pedagogical, not technological: **The goal of theological education is not just correct answers — it is formed thinkers.** From that premise they articulated a paired rule: **AI can assist research and structure**; **AI cannot replace the student’s own thinking, argument, or voice.**

Within a **semester**, the fog on syllabus day lifted: published AI guidelines for students and faculty; cross-course consistency; faculty language to justify classroom choices; student clarity about boundaries. The aim was never surveillance-first enforcement — it was **clarity**.

Students stopped guessing what each professor “really meant” by silence. Faculty stopped improvising contradictory rules that varied by mood and module. IT could support teaching rather than chase rumors.

### Step 02 · Sandbox — They explored carefully

Rather than wholesale ban or wholesale embrace, Westbridge staged **controlled inquiry**: using AI to summarize dense theological texts; generating outlines prior to drafting; comparing AI-generated arguments to student-written arguments — with **documentation and discussion** as part of the learning, not an afterthought.

They surfaced patterned truths: AI could help with scaffolding and clarity; it stumbled on theological nuance; it could mint **convincing but shallow** arguments. That empirical humility produced cultural capital — **a shared understanding of what AI could and could not do in their context.**

Sandbox work also detoxified shame. Students could admit where temptation lived; faculty could admit where curiosity lived; the institution could discuss tradeoffs without pretending purity or panic.

### Step 03 · Skills — They trained for formation, not just compliance

Training transcended plagiarism anxiety. Faculty folded AI into teaching conversations: When is it helpful? When does it shortcut formation? What does it mean to **own** an argument? Students practiced triangulation — how to use AI responsibly, how to critique outputs, how to recognize when convenience replaces cognition.

Students grew **more aware, not merely more restricted**: they could articulate when AI helped learning, when it harmed it, and how to choose deliberately. Faculty posture shifted in parallel — **from policing AI to guiding its use.**

Formation language stopped sounding like a thin veneer over surveillance. Conversations returned to virtues seminaries actually care about: patience, intellectual honesty, courage to revise, and the slow work of owning an argument.

### Step 04 · Solutions — They integrated tools thoughtfully

Only after clarity and formation-work did Westbridge wire tools into systems: a research assistant oriented to **approved** academic sources; writing support emphasizing **structure rather than ghostwritten content**; a faculty hub collating AI-informed pedagogy.

They explicitly avoided **automated paper-writing tools** and anything that **obscured authorship**. For the most formation-critical work, they avoided a tool-first answer altogether — opting instead for **a faculty guide on how to teach *with* AI — not merely how to detect it.**

The institution did not magically become “more efficient.” It became **more intentional**: students engaged more critically; faculty shared expectations; AI entered syllabi as a named conversation partner rather than a hidden shortcut.

Tools served pedagogy instead of replacing it: the research assistant shortened paths to vetted sources rather than inventing citations; writing support surfaced outlines and transitions rather than ghostwriting theology; the faculty hub spread experiments that worked — including assignments that failed — so colleagues learned faster than rumor allowed.

### What this actually produced — and why the order held

Westbridge did not “solve” AI — no honest school has. It did something more durable: **integrated emerging tools without losing the purpose of theological education.**

The obstacle was never merely GPT; it was what GPT exposed — fragile assumptions about learning, authorship, and the relationship between effort and formation. The remedy was neither blanket prohibition nor naive optimism; it was **sequence**: clarify purpose, explore carefully, train for judgment, **then** integrate tools.

---

## Revision note

When `case-study.*.ts` changes in the repo, update this document so the long-form narratives remain aligned with the structured source of truth.
